post.description: Create a user and return an access token. 409 if email already exists.

Authenticate and return an access token. 401 on bad credentials.

Layered throttling:
- slowapi per-IP (decorator above) blocks burst from one origin
- per-account counter (``is_login_locked_out``) caps total failures
  across all source IPs against one account — attacker can't spread
  credential-stuffing across an IP rotation pool to evade the limit

On a fresh successful login we also opportunistically rehash the
stored password (``verify_and_maybe_rehash``) so users on outdated
bcrypt rounds get upgraded silently the next time they log in.

Revoke the presented Bearer token by writing its ``jti`` to the denylist.

Returns ``{"revoked": true}`` even if the token was already revoked
or never had a ``jti`` claim — there's no useful distinction for
the caller, and reporting "you were already logged out" leaks state.

Tokens issued before the ``jti`` rollout simply can't be revoked
server-side; clients should still drop their copy. Those tokens
expire naturally inside the JWT lifetime window.

get.description: List the user's non-deleted recordings, newest first, cursor-paged.

patch.description:
Update mutable fields. ``name`` is editable for the row's whole
life; ``expected_chunk_count`` only while ``recording`` (409 after),
and never below the already-uploaded count (atomic shrink-guard).

delete.description: Soft-delete. Subsequent reads 404; media retained per retention.

get.description: Status, chunk progress, and (once finalized) the playback URL.

post.description:
Upload one chunk. ``sha256`` optional (verified if supplied).
Idempotent: a same-(index,bytes) re-upload → 200 ``duplicate=true``.

get.description:
Serve one chunk by index. Token-validated (no Bearer) so HLS
players fetch it directly via the manifest.

post.description:
Finalize. ``expected_chunk_count`` optional (asserted if given,
else derived). 409 ``{detail,missing,request_id}`` on gaps; 409
``no chunks uploaded`` on empty; idempotent on terminal.

get.description: Signed URLs + metadata for a finalized recording. 404 if not ready.

HLS manifest. Token-validated (no Bearer). Live (no ENDLIST)
until ``finalized``; ``no-cache`` so players re-fetch while growing.

post.description:
Append batched ``SPEAKER_CHANGE`` events. Seconds-since-start are
normalised to ``wall_clock_ms`` via ``client_started_at``; each push
is an immutable per-session-prefix object (race-safe, no RMW).

get.description: Return the authenticated user's profile (token-validity probe).

post.description:
Telemetry sink. No-op persistence today — logged at INFO so an
operator can grep by name; 204 drains the client buffer.

Upload the speaker-id JSONL sidecar for a session.

Idempotent: re-uploads for the same session overwrite the prior
blob + row. The desktop's retry behaviour assumes this — a transient
network failure that the client retries should not stack timelines.

The body is read once into memory (capped at 16 MiB), counted, then
streamed back to the storage backend. The counter is best-effort —
malformed lines are skipped rather than failing the upload, so a
transient encoding glitch on the client doesn't reject an otherwise
usable timeline. The correlation worker re-parses on read and
applies the same leniency.

get.description:
Return the transcript for a session.

Returns `status="queued"` with empty segments before the worker has run.
Segments are returned in `ord` order only when status == "ready".

Stream a file by its storage key, validated by a short-lived JWT in the query.

Unauthenticated by Bearer token — the token IS the auth, so QMediaPlayer
can hit this URL directly. 401 on bad/expired/wrong-key token.
Readiness probe — checks DB, Redis, and storage. 503 if any fails.

post.description:
Create a transcribe-session row + mint a WS token.

Validates:
  * Some provider is configured (API key set) and supports the
    requested language. The specific provider is server-picked
    and intentionally not surfaced to the caller.
  * User is under the concurrency cap.

Does NOT yet open the upstream provider WS — that happens lazily
inside the relay when the client connects to the stream endpoint.
Reasoning: the REST call is synchronous; opening a provider WS
here would add 100-300ms before responding, and a slow provider
handshake would 5xx the create call instead of producing a clean
error during streaming where the client can show it.

BotDispatchRequest.title: BotDispatchRequest
BotDispatchRequest.description:
Body of ``POST /api/v1/meetings/bot``.

The user names the meeting (cosmetic; the bot records this against
the dispatch so the user can identify it later in their list of
bot-recorded meetings), provides the join URL, and explicitly
picks the platform. We don't auto-detect from the URL host — the
Meet/Teams URL shapes have overlapping wildcards (e.g. corporate
custom domains for Teams), so a client-picked label removes that
ambiguity and lets the bot dispatcher route deterministically.

BotDispatchResponse.title: BotDispatchResponse
BotDispatchResponse.description:
Returned by ``POST /api/v1/meetings/bot`` on a successful
dispatch. ``bot_id`` is opaque to the extension — it's the dispatch
service's handle for the bot instance; the extension stores it so
a future "cancel bot" / "bot status" endpoint can reference it.

``status`` is one of:
  * ``"dispatched"`` — the bot service accepted the request and the
    bot is starting up.
  * ``"queued"`` — the dispatcher is at capacity; the bot will join
    when a slot frees up.

Errors surface as HTTP status codes, not in this body.

ChunkAccepted.title: ChunkAccepted
ChunkAccepted.description:
Echoes what was accepted. Same shape whether the chunk was newly
stored or an idempotent duplicate (the client treats any 2xx as
success and advances its queue).

MeResponse.title: MeResponse
MeResponse.description:
The extension's ``getMe()`` helper consumes only ``email`` today.

We include ``id`` + ``name`` for forward compatibility — future
extension features (display avatar, switch account UI) will read
them, and adding them server-side now means no second-round
deployment when the extension catches up.

SessionUpdate.title: SessionUpdate
SessionUpdate.description:
Partial update for an in-progress session.

Only fields that are explicitly present in the request body are
written — ``model_dump(exclude_unset=True)`` discriminates "the
client sent ``null``" from "the field wasn't in the JSON". Today
the only mutable fields are ``name`` (cosmetic) and
``expected_chunk_count`` (used by the chunk-upload range check
+ the status denominator).

SpeakerTimelineUploadResponse.title: SpeakerTimelineUploadResponse
SpeakerTimelineUploadResponse.description:
Diagnostic response to ``POST /sessions/{sid}/speaker_timeline``.

The desktop app does not consume any of these fields (it only checks
for non-2xx status), but they're useful for support / curl-driven
debugging and for the transcript route's optional inclusion of the
timeline status.

StartTranscribeRequest.title: StartTranscribeRequest
StartTranscribeRequest.description:
Body of ``POST /api/v1/transcribe/sessions``.

``language`` is BCP-47-ish (``en``, ``hi``, ``hi-IN``, ``auto``).
``auto`` requires a provider that detects language — currently
Soniox + Sarvam. The router rejects ``auto`` when the configured
server-side provider doesn't support it (422 so the caller
doesn't open a doomed WS).

``source_hint`` is purely diagnostic — the relay doesn't change
behaviour based on it. Useful for debugging which platform a
session came from when the live-transcribe overlay looks broken.

Note: the STT provider is NOT exposed here. The server picks one
based on which API keys are configured + the
``DEFAULT_TRANSCRIBE_PROVIDER`` env var; the client never sees
which vendor handles the audio. Same applies to provider
failover — server-armed when a fallback is configured, invisible
to the client.

StartTranscribeResponse.title: StartTranscribeResponse
StartTranscribeResponse.description:
201 reply. The extension reads ``ws_url`` and ``ws_token`` to
open the streaming socket; the rest is informational.

``sample_rate`` is fixed at 16000 — the AudioWorklet downsampler
on the extension side must emit at this rate. ``format`` is the
legacy alias kept for old clients (always ``pcm_s16le_mono``).

``audio_format`` is the new Phase-C field: the per-session wire
encoding the client should put on the WS. ``pcm_s16le`` (default)
means the AudioWorklet's raw Int16 output; ``opus`` means the
client should run the PCM through a WebCodecs ``AudioEncoder``
and ship raw Opus frames. The server picks based on the resolved
provider's ``wire_audio_format``.

The STT provider used is intentionally NOT in the response.
Server-controlled, hidden from the client.

TelemetryEvent.title: TelemetryEvent
TelemetryEvent.description:
The extension flushes one of these per buffered event.

``name`` is constrained to a small allow-list documented in
:py:mod:`extension/src/api/client.js`; we don't enforce that on the
backend so a future event name doesn't require a synchronized
extension + backend deploy. The payload is opaque JSON for the
same reason.

TimelineEvent.title: TimelineEvent
TimelineEvent.description:
One ``SPEAKER_CHANGE`` row buffered by the extension.

Time fields are seconds-since-recording-start as observed by the
content script's debounced ``startSpeakerDetector``; the backend
converts them to absolute ``wall_clock_ms`` for the correlator by
adding ``session.client_started_at``.

TimelinePushResponse.title: TimelinePushResponse
TimelinePushResponse.description:
Diagnostic. Extension treats any 2xx as success and drops its
local buffer.

TranscribeSessionStatus.title: TranscribeSessionStatus
TranscribeSessionStatus.description:
GET /api/v1/transcribe/sessions/{id} response.

Provider details (which vendor handled the session, whether
failover fired) are stored on the DB row for operations but NOT
returned here — the client has no visibility into vendor choice
per product policy.
